channel.title: Blockchain Games on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Blockchain Games on emsenn.net

item.title: Autonomous Worlds: Persistent Virtual Economies on Blockchain
item.description:
An autonomous world is a virtual environment built entirely on a public blockchain where game state, rules, and ownership persist independently of any single developer, company, or player. The world runs autonomously: its logic is encoded in smart contracts, its state is stored on-chain, and it continues to exist and be modifiable whether or not the original creators are active.

The vision, articulated most prominently by Ludens (co-founder of Lattice) in the August 2022 essay “Autonomous Worlds (Part 1),” inverts the traditional game development model. Instead of a studio building and controlling a game, a blockchain-based world becomes a shared, publicly-governed computational environment where players, developers, and communities collaborate and compete within a set of immutable rules.

item.title: Dark Forest: Pioneering On-Chain Gaming with Zero-Knowledge Proofs
item.description:
Dark Forest is an infinite, procedurally-generated, real-time strategy game running entirely on Ethereum. Players discover and conquer planets in a universe specified cryptographically. The radical innovation is that the game maintains incomplete information: each player knows the location of planets they have discovered, but the blockchain itself does not reveal coordinates to anyone, enforced using zero-knowledge proofs (zkSNARKs).

Launched in 2020 by Brian Gu (using the pseudonym Gubsheep) at MIT, Dark Forest demonstrated that fully on-chain gaming was possible and influenced the design of later frameworks like MUD. The game is named after Liu Cixin’s science fiction novel and operates under the principle that in a hostile universe, information asymmetry is your defense.

item.title: Entity Component System
item.description:
An Entity Component System (ECS) is a software architecture for games and simulations where:

  • Entities are containers (typically just unique identifiers) with no inherent properties
  • Components are data attached to entities (e.g., Position, Health, Velocity)
  • Systems are functions that operate on entities with specific components (e.g., MovementSystem reads Position and Velocity, writes new Position)

ECS uses composition over inheritance: instead of defining a class hierarchy (Player < Creature < Entity), every entity is just an ID, and behavior emerges from the set of components attached to it.

item.title: MUD Framework Architecture: Entity Component Systems on Ethereum
item.description:
MUD Framework Architecture

MUD is a framework for building multichain on-chain applications on any EVM-compatible blockchain. Its core innovation is applying the Entity Component System (ECS) architectural pattern—long established in game development—to Solidity smart contracts, with a central data storage layer that separates state from logic.

The framework consists of two main layers: Store, the low-level data storage engine with custom encoding, and World, which builds on Store to add access control, system registration, and delegation. Together, they enable developers to build complex, upgradeable on-chain applications where third-party developers can extend without forking.

item.title: MUD-Based Games: OPCraft, Sky Strife, and the Next Generation of Autonomous Worlds
item.description:
MUD-Based Games: OPCraft, Sky Strife, and the Next Generation

With the release of MUD v1 in late 2022, Lattice began building production games to validate the framework. These games—OPCraft, Sky Strife, and others—serve as both proof-of-concept and laboratories for autonomous world design, demonstrating what MUD enables and what challenges remain.

OPCraft: A Minecraft-Like On-Chain Universe

OPCraft was a voxel-based world deployed on Optimism (an Ethereum Layer 2), built by Lattice in just 1.5 months using MUD v1. The game is essentially Minecraft run on-chain: a 3D world composed of destructible/placeable blocks where players can mine, build, and craft.

item.title: Zero-Knowledge Proof
item.description:
A zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) is a cryptographic protocol in which one party (the prover) convinces another party (the verifier) that a statement is true without revealing any information used to construct the proof.

Formally, a ZKP protocol has three properties:

  1. Completeness — If the statement is true, the prover can construct a proof that the verifier will accept
  2. Soundness — If the statement is false, the prover cannot construct a valid proof (except with negligible probability)
  3. Zero-knowledge — The verifier learns nothing except that the statement is true; the proof reveals no additional information

Example: Knowing a Discrete Logarithm

Suppose Alice claims to know a value x such that g^x = y (where g and y are public). Alice wants to prove she knows x without revealing x.
